entities.description: The page-image production layer from which U-M created its 2006 OCR. The XML header describes 454 600 dpi TIFF G4 images and preserves 448 exact TIFF references through Arnold's printed p.412, but a current image endpoint or complete bundle was not verified.
entities.label: Historical U-M page-image set for AFB3713.0001.001
notes: The XML links 448 image filenames, 00000001.tif through 00000448.tif. Target printed pp.1–412 map to sequences 37–448; sequences 1–36 are title or preliminary matter.
The header's 454-image extent exceeds the 448 linked PB elements by six images. Their surfaces and filenames are not represented in the checked XML and remain unresolved.
This historical entity records an evidenced production layer; survival_status unknown means that current retrievability of the TIFF set has not been established.

bibliographic.citation: John Wyclif, Select English Works of John Wyclif, edited by Thomas Arnold, vol. I, Sermons on the Gospels for Sundays and Festivals (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1869).
bibliographic.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif

entities.description: Arnold's edition of 123 Middle English sermons in three sections: Evangelia Dominicalia, Commune Sanctorum, and Proprium Sanctorum. Arnold treated them as Wyclif's, but modern scholarship attributes the long cycle more cautiously to anonymous Wycliffite author or authors.
entities.label: Thomas Arnold's 1869 Select English Works, volume I
notes: The target medieval text occupies printed pp.1–412: fifty-four Sunday-Gospel sermons on pp.1–163, thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons on pp.165–294, and thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons on pp.295–412.

Arnold calls Bodley 788 his complete and accurate base and says he reproduced its orthography almost exactly while regulating u/v and i/j by sound, retaining thorn and yogh, and supplying capitals, paragraphing, punctuation, marginal analyses, and biblical references.
W. F. Cornish prepared the transcript through printed p.197; William Sorell prepared the remainder. Arnold was responsible for editorial selection, collation, and presentation.

rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1869, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Arnold's 1869 edited text, introduction, notes, apparatus, and arrangement
notes: This U.S. conclusion does not itself determine every non-U.S. jurisdiction or rights in modern scans.

entities.description: Arnold's continuous base manuscript for all 123 sermons in the volume-I selection, an English parchment codex of the late fourteenth century. Arnold called it complete and accurate.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 788
notes: Arnold's transcription relationship is continuous across the exact 123-sermon selection, unlike his selected apparatus use of other witnesses.
English manuscript with illuminated capitals.

entities.description: An English parchment manuscript dated 1400–1440 that contains Sunday Gospels, Commune Sanctorum, and Proprium Sanctorum. Arnold's apparatus explicitly reports three D readings, although the current British Library catalog says the manuscript was lettered D but not collated by Arnold.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 40672
notes: The D relation is restricted to the three readings actually visible in Arnold's apparatus and does not assert systematic collation.
Sunday Gospels begin at f.55, Commune Sanctorum at f.107, and Proprium Sanctorum at f.150b.

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Douce 321

entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century parchment manuscript and important independent witness used for selected E readings across all three Arnold sections. Arnold records missing leaves spanning part of the Proprium section and the absence of Sermon CIV.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 321
notes: Arnold says two leaves are missing across material corresponding to printed p.302 through a phrase on p.306; Sermon CIV is absent. Those gaps limit E's coverage but do not make the whole witness lost.
Written in double columns.
The cataloged sermon contents include Sunday material, saints' material, and ferial material.

next_steps: Consult the complete Hudson–Gradon five-volume edition and later scholarship rather than using Arnold's attribution as the critical baseline.
Separate evidence for cycle unity from evidence for one author, several authors, revision, or compilation.
Retain manuscript and edition attributions as reception evidence without converting them into confirmed authorship.
openQuestions.question: What single-author, collaborative, compilatory, or revision model best explains the unified 294-sermon long cycle, and what relationship did John Wyclif have to its composition?

next_steps: Review dating arguments across all five Hudson–Gradon volumes and later textual scholarship.
Reassess Arnold's Richard II and persecution evidence independently of his authorship conclusion.
Keep witness production dates distinct from composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the exact 123-sermon selection or its three constituent sections be dated more narrowly without depending on Wyclif's personal authorship or transferring dates from surviving copies?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the catalog statement means not systematically collated rather than never consulted.
Inspect Arnold's surviving papers, collations, or proof materials if identified.
Retain the lineage edge at the three printed readings only unless stronger evidence appears.
notes: The conflict does not justify deleting the printed D readings or expanding them into a systematic collation.
openQuestions.question: How should Arnold's three explicit D apparatus readings be reconciled with the current British Library statement that Wrest Park 11 / Add MS 40672 was lettered D but not collated by Arnold?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Imaging Services to confirm microfilm 177(2), its exact folio coverage, format, and current delivery options.
Prefer a new whole-codex color facsimile if available, including binding, endleaves, blanks, and all folios.
Do not model the unverified microfilm as an extant facsimile entity until coverage and access are confirmed.
openQuestions.question: Does Bodleian Manuscript Photographs microfilm 177(2), cited by MEC for Douce 321, cover the entire manuscript, and how can it currently be accessed or ordered?

next_steps: Ask each holding institution whether an unindexed or reading-room surrogate exists.
For Bodley 788, distinguish the verified ten-image fols.1r–5v selection from a whole-codex facsimile.
If ordering images, request the whole manuscript where practical and retain an exact work-portion map inside the full object.
Update this dated negative finding if a public IIIF manifest, complete viewer, or downloadable surrogate is found.
openQuestions.question: Are complete public digital facsimiles available for Bodley 788, CUL Ii.1.40, Laud Misc.314, Add MS 40672, Douce 321, Royal 18 B IX, or Cotton Claudius D VIII beyond the sources checked here?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections whether the historical linked images remain retrievable and whether they derive from the same physical copy as the Internet Archive scan.
Compare image identifiers and page geometry if a current image route becomes available.
Keep the encoding-to-Arnold relation independent of an unverified current U-M facsimile entity.
openQuestions.question: What exact page-image set and digitization workflow produced U-M item AFB3713.0001.001, and is a stable whole-volume image download still available?

reviewNotes: Primary transmission is modeled as repository artifact → U-M encoding → historical U-M page-image set → Arnold edition → Bodley 788 base witness, with selected collation edges to B, C, D, and E.
Royal 18 B IX and Cotton Claudius D VIII are supporting authenticity checks for narrowly identified passages, not general textual bases or systematic collations.
Trinity B.2.17 and B.4.20 are supporting witnesses consulted or described by Arnold; their complete IIIF bundles do not imply that their readings entered the edited text.
Modern Hudson–Gradon and Ghosh scholarship informs work identity and attribution but is not represented as an upstream transmission ancestor.

lineage.summary: The checked-in afb3713 XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item AFB3713.0001.001, an uncorrected 2006 OCR derived from Thomas Arnold's 1869 Select English Works of John Wyclif, volume I. The target is Arnold's bounded 123-sermon selection—fifty-four Sunday-Gospel sermons, thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons, and thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons—from the unified 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle, not the whole cycle and not John Wyclif's oeuvre. Arnold printed Bodleian MS Bodley 788 as his continuous base, used selected variants from Cambridge Ii.1.40, Bodleian Laud Misc.314, Wrest Park 11 (now British Library Add MS 40672), and Bodleian Douce 321, checked only three identified passages against Royal 18 B IX and Cotton Claudius D VIII, and described or examined Trinity B.2.17 and B.4.20 without supplying identified apparatus readings from them. The local research cache preserves a complete Arnold scan, a partial Bodley 788 image selection, complete provider sequences for both Trinity manuscripts, and exact institutional catalog and rights evidence; no complete public facsimile was found for the base manuscript or the six other non-Trinity witnesses as of 2026-07-15.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Wycliffite author or authors
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Arnold selection published 1869
catalogSummary.region: England; Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture
catalogSummary.title: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals

catalogingSubject.label: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is the exact 123-sermon Middle English selection printed in Thomas Arnold's 1869 volume I and encoded as U-M item AFB3713.0001.001: fifty-four Sunday Gospel sermons, thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons, and thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons. These are three liturgically coherent portions of the unified 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle, not 123 separately attributed works and not the whole corpus of writings historically assigned to John Wyclif. Arnold's title page, introduction, contents, marginal analyses, biblical references, notes, supplied punctuation, and other apparatus remain edition-level matter rather than medieval component works.

contentParts.label: Evangelia Dominicalia: Sunday Gospels
contentParts.summary: Fifty-four sermons on Sunday Gospel readings, ordered from the first Sunday after Trinity through Trinity Sunday.

contentParts.label: Commune Sanctorum
contentParts.summary: Thirty-one sermons, numbered LV-LXXXV, for Gospel readings in the Common of Saints.

contentParts.label: Proprium Sanctorum
contentParts.summary: Thirty-eight sermons, numbered LXXXVI-CXXIII, for saints' days and other festivals from St Andrew's vigil through All Saints' Day.

assertion.notes: The deliberately broad range does not depend on Arnold's assumption of Wyclif's personal authorship or transfer a witness-copying date to composition.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century
notes: Arnold's narrower arguments from Richard II and persecution references belong to the history of attribution and dating, not a settled modern composition date.

scope.description: First publication of Arnold's exact volume-I edited selection

extent.description: The medieval selection comprises 54 Sunday Gospel sermons, 31 Commune Sanctorum sermons, and 38 Proprium Sanctorum sermons. The complete XML has five DIV1 elements (two omitted-front-matter containers and three medieval content sections), 123 DIV2, 252 HEAD, 499 P, 448 PB, 798 NOTE1 plus three header NOTE elements, five LG, and twenty-six L. All five line groups and all twenty-six line elements are modern editorial quotations inside Arnold's notes, so the medieval selection remains prose.

formStatements.description: All 123 medieval sermons are prose. The XML's five LG and twenty-six L elements occur inside Arnold's modern editorial notes and do not make the cataloged selection mixed-form.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin Gospel lemmata, rubrics, and short phrases are embedded formulae and are not promoted to a separate work-level language facet.
The source header's broad three-volume bibliographic description is inherited catalog metadata; the encoded title page and content identify the exact immediate source as Arnold volume I (1869).

The XML preserves page milestones for twenty-nine omitted introductory and contents pages but does not transcribe their wording; those omissions are edition-layer boundaries, not missing medieval sermons.
The XML title-page attribution to John Wyclif is edition-derived and is not treated as proof of authorship.
The record remains partial because authorship and narrower composition dating remain unresolved.

next_steps: Consult the complete Hudson-Gradon five-volume edition and later scholarship rather than using Arnold's attribution as the critical baseline.
Separate evidence for cycle unity from evidence for one author, several authors, revision, or compilation.
Retain manuscript and edition attributions as reception evidence without converting them into confirmed authorship.
openQuestions.question: What single-author, collaborative, compilatory, or revision model best explains the unified 294-sermon long cycle, and what relationship did John Wyclif have to its composition?

next_steps: Review the textual and historical dating arguments across all five Hudson-Gradon volumes.
Reassess Arnold's Richard II and persecution evidence independently of his authorship conclusion.
Keep witness production dates distinct from composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the three selected sermon sections be dated more narrowly without depending on Wyclif's personal authorship or transferring dates from surviving copies?

notes: Witness origins, scribal dialects, and present repositories are not transferred automatically to the work's place of composition.
Cycle unity is supported, but the reviewed evidence does not resolve single versus collaborative authorship.

scope.description: All 123 sermons as selected from the unified long cycle

assertion.notes: Supported as reception and edition history, not as confirmed authorship.

notes: Arnold argued for Wyclif's authorship; current scholarship instead describes a cycle produced by Wyclif's followers and leaves its authorship unresolved.

scope.description: Historical attribution of all three sermon sections
The complete 1869 volume-I selection and editorial apparatus
Arnold printed pp. 1-197: all Sunday Gospel sermons and Commune Sanctorum through the beginning of Sermon LXIII
Arnold printed pp. 198-412: the remainder of Commune Sanctorum and all Proprium Sanctorum
Selected variants and corrections from manuscripts B, C, D, and E, plus passage checks in Royal 18 B IX and Cotton Claudius D VIII

summaries.value: This 123-sermon selection expounds Gospel readings for Sundays, the Common of Saints, and the Proper of Saints in Middle English. Sermons normally begin from a Latin Gospel lemma, explain its narrative and spiritual senses, and apply scripture to belief, sin and virtue, preaching, pastoral conduct, wealth and poverty, sacraments, clergy and friars, papal authority, Antichrist, and reform of the church. The three sections belong to the larger 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle.

Part I contains fifty-four Sunday Gospel sermons (Arnold I-LIV, pp. 1-163). Part II contains thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons (LV-LXXXV, pp. 165-294). Part III contains thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons (LXXXVI-CXXIII, pp. 295-412).

The record preserves Arnold's volume-I boundary because afb3713 encodes that exact selection, while keeping the intellectual cycle, manuscript witnesses, 1869 edition, U-M encoding, and checked-in XML distinct. John Wyclif remains a disputed attributed author rather than the confirmed creator.

notes: A supplied modern title that combines the established cycle name with the exact Arnold volume-I boundary; it is not claimed as a medieval rubric.
titles.value: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals

notes: The immediate edition title preserves Arnold's historical attribution; it is not adopted as proof of Wyclif's authorship.

Sermons normally begin from a Latin Gospel lemma, explain its narrative and spiritual senses, and apply scripture to belief, sin and virtue, preaching, pastoral conduct, wealth and poverty, sacraments, clergy and friars, papal authority, Antichrist, and reform of the church. The three sections belong to the larger 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle.
